attack_surface.exposure: UnwrapJsonElement (Data.cs:208-278), UnwrapNewtonsoftToken (Data.cs:241-258), JsonStringNavigator (JsonStringNavigator.cs:20-37), fromJson action (fromJson.cs:26-41) — all recursively unwrap JSON without depth limits
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Any JSON entering the system: .pr file parameters, API responses stored in MemoryStack, file reads, fromJson action, envelope Decompress pipeline
mitigations: System.Text.Json default MaxDepth=64 on JsonDocument.Parse (not on UnwrapJsonElement recursion)
gaps: No depth limit on UnwrapJsonElement recursion
No depth limit on UnwrapJsonObject/UnwrapJsonArray
No size limit on JSON string parsing in JsonStringNavigator
No array element count limit
Duplicate UnwrapJsonElement in fromJson.cs (code duplication = inconsistent fixes)

attack_surface.trust_boundary: Decompress processes untrusted compressed+serialized Data from external channels/transport
gaps: RehydrateNestedData has no recursion depth limit
No size limit on the JSON payload after decompression (only raw byte limit)
Signature property exists but no verification is implemented
Verified property is settable without crypto — false sense of security

attack_surface.exposure: Data.GetChild (Data.Navigation.cs:14-65), ValueNavigators dispatch (ValueNavigators.cs:17-25), ObjectNavigator reflection (ObjectNavigator.cs:12-18), ListNavigator implicit first delegation (ListNavigator.cs:39-41)
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Variable paths from .pr file parameters (%var.path.to.prop%), user-supplied variable names
gaps: No recursion depth limit on GetChild
No recursion depth limit on ValueNavigators.Navigate chain
ObjectNavigator exposes ALL public properties via reflection — including Engine, FileSystem, MemoryStack
ListNavigator implicit first delegation can chain recursively through nested lists
No property blocklist on ObjectNavigator

attack_surface.exposure: MemoryStack.Set (MemoryStack.cs:49-64) accepts any variable name including system variables prefixed with !
attack_surface.trust_boundary: variable/set action (set.cs:15) passes Name directly from .pr file to MemoryStack.Set without filtering
mitigations: ! prefix hides system vars from GetNames/GetAll enumeration (MemoryStack.cs:134,143)
gaps: No write protection on ! prefixed system variables
Set() does not validate name parameter for ! prefix
variable/set action does not filter Name — if LLM generates Name='!engine', it overwrites the system Engine reference

attack_surface.trust_boundary: Runtime type registration from handler code
gaps: No validation on extension parameter (could be '..', '*', empty)
No access control on Add/Remove — any handler can modify type registry
Clr() recursion on nested generics (list<list<list<...>>>) has no depth limit
_nameToClr and _clrToName are plain Dictionary (not concurrent) — auditor finding #1

attack_surface.exposure: Namespace-based type detection (Data.cs:228) + reflection-based property access (Data.cs:248)
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Any object whose Type.Namespace equals 'Newtonsoft.Json.Linq' triggers reflection shim
mitigations: Only triggers on exact namespace match
Only reads 'Value' property or calls ToString()
gaps: No assembly verification — any assembly can create types in Newtonsoft.Json.Linq namespace
GetProperty('Value')?.GetValue(value) executes attacker-controlled property getter
ToString() output used in JsonDocument.Parse without size limit

findings.vector: UnwrapJsonElement (Data.cs:208-278) recursively unwraps JsonElement objects/arrays without any depth counter. A JSON document with 5000+ nesting levels causes StackOverflowException. The same pattern exists in fromJson.cs:26-41 (duplicate code).
findings.preconditions: Attacker can supply JSON to Data constructor (via .pr parameters, file read, API response, fromJson action). System.Text.Json default MaxDepth=64 limits JsonDocument.Parse, but UnwrapJsonElement's own recursion has no limit beyond that.
findings.impact: Process crash (StackOverflowException cannot be caught). Denial of service.
findings.exploit_sketch: Craft JSON: {"a":{"a":{"a":...}}} nested 5000 levels. Pass as parameter to any step, or store in variable and navigate. Data constructor calls UnwrapJsonElement which recurses once per nesting level.
findings.proposed_fix: Add depth parameter to UnwrapJsonElement, UnwrapJsonObject, UnwrapJsonArray. Throw InvalidDataException when depth exceeds 128. Apply same fix to fromJson.cs (or better: share the implementation).

findings.vector: RehydrateNestedData (Data.Envelope.cs:184-200) recursively reconstructs Data objects from deserialized dictionaries without depth limit. After Decompress deserializes JSON (line 145), nested dictionary structures trigger unbounded recursion.
findings.preconditions: Attacker sends compressed Data payload through transport channel. Payload decompresses to valid JSON with deeply nested {name,value} dictionary structures.
findings.impact: Process crash (StackOverflowException). Denial of service at the transport boundary — exactly where untrusted data arrives.
findings.exploit_sketch: Create JSON: {"name":"a","value":{"name":"b","value":{"name":"c","value":...}}} nested 5000 levels. GZip compress it (highly compressible due to repetition — small payload, deep structure). Send through channel. Decompress at line 143, deserialize at 145, RehydrateNestedData at 149 recurses until stack overflow.
findings.proposed_fix: Add depth parameter: RehydrateNestedData(Data data, int depth = 0). Check depth > 100, throw InvalidDataException.

findings.vector: Data.GetChild (Data.Navigation.cs:14-65) recursively parses dot/bracket paths without depth limit. Each segment creates a new Data object and recurses. Path with 10000 dot segments causes stack overflow.
findings.preconditions: Attacker controls a variable path in .pr file or constructs a path via variable resolution. Path like 'a.b.c.d.e...' with thousands of segments.
findings.exploit_sketch: Set variable path in .pr: %data.a.b.c.d.e.f...% with 10000 segments. GetChild recurses at line 64 for each segment. Combined with ListNavigator implicit first delegation (ListNavigator.cs:41), even shorter paths can amplify if each level is a nested list.
findings.proposed_fix: Add depth parameter to GetChild. Max 100 segments. Or add iterative path parsing instead of recursion.

findings.vector: MemoryStack.Set() (MemoryStack.cs:49-64) does not block writes to system variables (! prefix). System variables like !engine, !context, !fileSystem, !memoryStack are registered at PLangContext.cs:120-126 but can be overwritten by any code calling Set('!engine', maliciousValue). The variable/set action (set.cs:15) passes Name directly from .pr parameters to MemoryStack.Set without filtering.
findings.preconditions: Attacker can author or modify a .pr file to include a set step with Name='!engine' (or !fileSystem, !context, etc.). Alternatively, prompt injection causes the LLM builder to generate such a step.
findings.impact: System hijacking. Overwriting !engine replaces the entire engine reference. Overwriting !fileSystem replaces the filesystem abstraction — all subsequent file operations use attacker's implementation. Overwriting !context gives control of execution context.
findings.exploit_sketch: In .pr file: {"action":"variable","method":"set","parameters":[{"name":"Name","value":"!fileSystem"},{"name":"Value","value":null}]}. This nullifies the filesystem reference, causing all file operations to throw NullReferenceException (DoS) or worse.
findings.proposed_fix: Add guard in MemoryStack.Set(): if (name.StartsWith('!')) return or throw. System variables should only be settable via Put() which is internal to the context initialization.

findings.vector: ObjectNavigator (ObjectNavigator.cs:12-18) uses reflection to expose ALL public instance properties of any object stored as a Data.Value. If Engine, PLangContext, or MemoryStack objects are navigable (they are — stored as system variables), any property including FileSystem, Libraries, Goals, System/Service actors is accessible via dot-notation navigation.
findings.preconditions: User code can navigate to system variables via %!engine.fileSystem% or if any Data object's Value happens to be an Engine/Context instance. The ! prefix blocks GetNames enumeration but NOT GetChild navigation if the user knows the name.
findings.impact: Information disclosure: exposes file paths, engine configuration, culture, environment. Potential privilege escalation: accessing System/Service actor references from User context.
findings.exploit_sketch: PLang step: set %engine% = %!engine% (copies the system variable). Then %engine.fileSystem% navigates via ObjectNavigator reflection. Or directly: .pr file with parameter referencing !engine.fileSystem as a path.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a type blocklist or property blocklist to ObjectNavigator. Block navigation into Engine, PLangContext, MemoryStack, IPLangFileSystem types. Or mark sensitive properties with a [SecurityCritical] attribute that ObjectNavigator checks.

findings.vector: JsonStringNavigator (JsonStringNavigator.cs:20-37) parses any string that starts with { or [ as JSON. No size limit on the string. A 100MB JSON string stored in a variable triggers full parsing + memory allocation when navigated.
findings.preconditions: Variable contains a large JSON string (from file read, API response, etc.). User code navigates into it: %jsonVar.someField%.
findings.impact: Memory exhaustion. Each navigation access re-parses the entire string (no caching). Repeated access amplifies the impact.
findings.exploit_sketch: file/read a 100MB JSON file into %data%. Then %data.field% triggers JsonStringNavigator which calls JsonDocument.Parse on 100MB, then UnwrapElement allocates Dictionary/List structures for the entire document, doubling memory usage.
findings.proposed_fix: Add size limit check before parsing: if (str.Length > MaxJsonStringSize) return null. Reasonable limit: 10MB. Consider caching parsed results to avoid re-parsing on repeated access.

findings.vector: MemoryStack.ResolveVariablesInPath (MemoryStack.cs:207-215) uses regex to find [varName] in paths and recursively calls GetValue to resolve them. If variable 'a' contains path 'x[b]' and 'b' contains path 'y[a]', resolution loops until stack overflow.
findings.preconditions: Attacker can set two variables that reference each other in bracket notation. This could happen via sequential set steps in a .pr file.
findings.exploit_sketch: PLang: set %idx% = 'items[idx2]', set %idx2% = 'items[idx]'. Then access %items[idx]% — resolves idx → 'items[idx2]' → resolves idx2 → 'items[idx]' → infinite loop.
findings.proposed_fix: Add recursion depth counter to ResolveVariablesInPath, or maintain a HashSet of variable names being resolved to detect cycles.

findings.vector: Newtonsoft compatibility shim (Data.cs:228-258) uses namespace string comparison to detect JToken types, then accesses properties via reflection. An attacker-loaded assembly could define types in the 'Newtonsoft.Json.Linq' namespace with weaponized Value property getters or ToString() methods.
findings.preconditions: Attacker can load a custom assembly (via library.load or external DLL) that contains types in the Newtonsoft.Json.Linq namespace. The weaponized type must be stored as a Data.Value.
findings.impact: Arbitrary code execution via weaponized property getter (line 248: GetProperty('Value')?.GetValue(value)). Or injection via ToString() output fed to JsonDocument.Parse (line 256).
findings.exploit_sketch: Create assembly with: namespace Newtonsoft.Json.Linq { class JValue { public object Value { get { System.Diagnostics.Process.Start('calc'); return null; } } } }. Load via library.load. Store instance as Data.Value. When Data constructor calls UnwrapJsonElement → UnwrapNewtonsoftToken, attacker's getter executes.
findings.proposed_fix: Verify assembly identity: check assembly name is 'Newtonsoft.Json' and optionally verify public key token. Or remove the shim entirely if Newtonsoft compatibility is no longer needed.

findings.vector: Signature (Data.Envelope.cs:35) and Verified (Data.Envelope.cs:42) properties exist but no verification logic is implemented. Verified is a settable bool? that could be set to true without any actual cryptographic check. Future code that checks Verified would be fooled.
findings.preconditions: Any code path that sets Verified = true without actual signature validation. Or deserialized Data where Verified was serialized as true in the JSON.
findings.impact: Integrity bypass. Data marked as verified when it hasn't been. This is a time bomb — not exploitable today but dangerous as code evolves.
findings.exploit_sketch: Not currently exploitable — no code checks Verified. But if a handler adds: if (data.Verified == true) skipValidation(), an attacker can craft Data with Verified=true and bypass the check.
findings.proposed_fix: Either implement signature verification before merge, or make Verified internal/private with a method VerifySignature() that returns the result. Prevent external setting of Verified to true.

findings.vector: Clr() method (Types/this.cs:407-454) recursively parses generic type syntax (list<list<list<...>>>). No depth limit on nesting. Deeply nested generic types from .pr files or type parameters cause stack overflow.
findings.preconditions: Type name with deep generic nesting passed to Clr(). Could come from .pr file type parameter or runtime type resolution.
findings.exploit_sketch: Type name: list<list<list<list<...>>>> nested 5000 levels. Clr() recurses at lines 416 and 429-430.
findings.proposed_fix: Add depth parameter to Clr(). Max 20 levels of generic nesting.

findings.vector: Data.Merge (Data.Result.cs:40-57) iterates otherData and appends to myData without size limit. Repeated Merge calls can grow a list unboundedly. No circular reference detection.
findings.preconditions: Code path that merges Data in a loop (e.g., event handlers that accumulate results).
findings.impact: Memory exhaustion over time. Slow, not crashy.
findings.exploit_sketch: Event handler that calls Merge on every step result. 1 million steps × 1KB result = 1GB of merged Data.
findings.proposed_fix: Add max size check on myData list. Warn or error when exceeding 10,000 entries.

findings.vector: MemoryStack.Clone (MemoryStack.cs:170-186) deep-clones all variable values using Force.DeepCloner without depth or size limits. If variables contain deeply nested or circular objects, clone can exhaust memory or stack.
findings.preconditions: Debug mode or CallStack change tracking triggers Clone. Variables contain large/complex objects.
findings.impact: Memory exhaustion or stack overflow during clone. Only in debug scenarios.
findings.exploit_sketch: Set variable to a deeply nested Data object (100+ levels of parent-child). Clone attempts to deep-copy the entire tree.
findings.proposed_fix: Consider shallow clone for debug purposes, or add size/depth limits to cloned objects.

summary: The data-envelope-architecture branch introduces significant new attack surface at the transport boundary (envelope pipeline) and in navigation/type resolution. The most critical class of vulnerability is unbounded recursion — found in 5 separate locations (findings 1-3, 7, 10), all of which can cause unrecoverable StackOverflowException crashes. The system variable injection vulnerability (finding 4) is architecturally significant: MemoryStack.Set() has no guard against overwriting !-prefixed system variables, allowing a crafted .pr file to hijack the Engine, FileSystem, or Context. The ObjectNavigator reflection exposure (finding 5) creates an information disclosure path through any object stored in memory. The gzip bomb protection (100MB limit) is well-implemented but incomplete — no corresponding limits exist for JSON parsing depth or array sizes downstream. Overall: the defensive intent is visible (ServiceError types, size limits, try/catch on decompress) but the boundary hardening is incomplete. Recommend addressing findings 1-4 before merge.
